title: SEGMENT 6 — The cell cycle and how cancer breaks it

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — The cell cycle and how cancer breaks it

Let me back up and tell you what cells normally do when they divide, because cancer is, at heart, a corruption of this process, and you can't see what's broken until you see what's supposed to happen.
Imagine a factory assembly line.
A raw chassis comes in at one end; a finished car drives out the other.
In between, the chassis moves through a sequence of stations, each one adding something — wheels, doors, engine, paint, electronics.
And critically, between certain stations there are inspectors who check the work so far.
If something is wrong, the inspector pulls a cord that pauses the entire line until the problem is fixed.
If the problem can't be fixed, the inspector orders the chassis junked and the line restarts.
No defective car ever makes it to the dealership.
That, structurally, is the cell cycle.
The cell cycle has four phases that loop.
G1 is the first growth phase — the cell grows in size, makes proteins, and decides whether to commit to dividing.
S phase is where the cell copies its entire genome — every base of three billion base pairs has to be duplicated, faithfully.
G2 is a second growth phase — the cell checks the copying job and prepares for division.
And finally M phase, mitosis — the actual physical splitting of one cell into two.
Then the daughter cells either re-enter G1 to go around again, or exit into a quiet, non-dividing state called G0.
Between these phases sit the checkpoints — the inspectors.
The most famous is the G1/S checkpoint, sometimes called the restriction point.
This is the checkpoint that asks: "Are we really sure we want to start copying the genome?
Once we start, we're committed.
" Another sits between G2 and M and asks: "Did the copying go correctly?
Is the DNA intact and ready for division?
" And another sits during M phase itself and asks: "Are the chromosomes properly attached to the spindle apparatus before we actually pull them apart?
The proteins that push the cell forward through these checkpoints — the foremen who say "go to the next station" — are called cyclin-dependent kinases, or CDKs.
Each phase has its own CDK paired with a regulatory partner called a cyclin.
Cyclin D pairs with CDK4 and CDK6 to push the cell through G1.
Cyclin E with CDK2 to commit to S phase.
Cyclin A and Cyclin B with other CDKs to govern S, G2, and M.
Think of cyclins as time-limited keys that unlock each CDK in sequence.
When the key is present, the foreman is active and the line advances; when the key gets destroyed, the foreman goes idle and the line waits.
Now — what does the brake look like in this picture?
The most important G1 brake is our friend RB.
RB physically grabs onto a set of transcription factors called E2F, holding them prisoner so they can't activate the genes needed for S phase.
To enter S phase, the cell must release E2F, and the way it does that is by phosphorylating RB — sticking phosphate groups onto it, which changes its shape and makes it let go.
Who does the phosphorylating?
Cyclin D paired with CDK4/6.
So the sequence is: the cell decides it's time to divide, cyclin D rises, cyclin D plus CDK4/6 phosphorylate RB, RB releases E2F, E2F turns on the S phase genes, and the cell commits.
Now you can see what cancer does to this picture.
Cancer breaks the checkpoint controls.
Lots of cancers either have too much cyclin D, or too much CDK4/6 activity, or — most simply — no RB at all.
Any of those breaks the gate at the same place: RB doesn't hold E2F back, E2F runs free, the cell barges through into S phase whether the conditions are right or not.
Knowing this, we can be clever.
If a cancer cell is hyperactive because it has too much CDK4/6 driving things, can we just drug CDK4/6?
Yes — and we have.
Drugs called CDK4/6 inhibitors — palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib — are now standard treatment for certain breast cancers.
They put a brake back on the foreman.
They only work if the cancer still has functional RB downstream, because the whole logic is "stop CDK4/6 so RB stays in charge"; if there's no RB to be in charge, the drug has nothing to leverage.
So as we use these drugs we screen tumors for whether RB is still there.
The G2/M checkpoint, similarly, is where p53 sometimes intervenes when DNA damage is detected.
If the inspector finds the chassis dented, p53 either holds the line until repairs are done, or — if the dents are too bad — orders the chassis junked.
Cancer cells with no p53 don't have that quality control, which is why they accumulate gross DNA damage that would have killed a normal cell long ago.
So the cell cycle is the assembly line.
CDKs and cyclins are the foremen.
RB is the gatekeeper at the most important inspection station.
p53 is the senior quality inspector with the authority to junk a defective chassis.
Cancer breaks the foremen, the gatekeepers, and the inspectors, in various combinations, so that defective cells roll off the line that should never have been built.
